item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Big fan of Godel, transfinite numbers in set theory, and fractal metaversal conceptions. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of the short story "The Egg" in a very good way. This, like it, is a beautiful story.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a point of contention among many these days, but in most cases here I think sentience fits better. In most cases in sci-fi I see sentience used to mean "the ability to process and utilize information" in a general, abstract sense. Sapience however is usually used something like "complex sentience with reasoning abilities"

The article here describes an increase in both reasoning ability and sensory processing, expansion of senses, ect. So I think sentience, which today has expanded to have a very broad meaning is probably the better term. This is my opinion though and maybe I'm way off base.

Either way thanks for bringing it to my attention because its absolutely something to think about

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: English is not my native language so I may be missing a thing or two here, but I believe you keep using "sentient" (i.e. having feelings and perception) instead of "sapient" (i.e. able to think and reason) throughout the text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yeah pretty clear to me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It sounded like epsilon-naught, what is the difference between the two?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It didn't get stated very well in the final version I think but the implication is that "the oracle" is a title that gets passed around as council members die, that portion of the article was written when it was O5-2, and the current oracle is O5-9.

Thanks for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The credentials you input are or O5-9…but it says its for O5-2 eyes only. Is that intentional and this info is getting out and going to ruin everything? or is that a typo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this one was almost full of more jargon than I could comprehend, but I made it through in the end
I fucking love this concept. I don't have the words to properly articulate why, but I love theories of creation above all else, and this is one of the most interesting I've ever seen.

I love the idea that all of reality is essentially a bootstrap paradox — we created God, and God created us, but if we both created each other, where did we come from in the first place?

needless to say, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That… hurt my brain.
This is amazing.
I always admire SCPs with a multiversal concept, but this one pushes it the extra mile.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for this, that's really, really what I was hoping at least someone would get from it, that's what I got from writing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this at midnight so it took me two reads to fully get it. Maybe I'm either missing or projecting something, but there's an elegant sort of beauty to this one that compels my upvote, the same "drifting under a starlit ocean" feeling I momentarily get from looking at those Wikipedia images about voids and how big the universe really is.

Or maybe I'm just rambling. I'm bleh on new classes, but I'll still fork over the +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do kinda like the concept of a species becoming as knowledgeable as they possibly could and using that knowledge to create a new universe that sustains life that could know more than their predecessors, but I'm not super fond of the execution.

The new object class seems unnecessary to me from both an in universe and out of universe standpoint. I really think your article would have been fine if this was either given a standard object class or classed as explained.

The whole "consent for cognitive analysis" thing in the interview log disrupted the flow of the article for me. I feel you could have shortened this or just gone without. The interview log also pretty much only features this and a big exposition dump at the end, which felt somewhat contrived to me.

I also could have done without anything that happens under the collapsible. I find the concept and possible story you set up in the interview to be far more interesting than anything that happened in the collapsible.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a little confused while reading this at first but I got to the end and whoa. +1 for sure

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Science Fiction is fine. The site has a lot of articles that delve into Sci-Fi themes, as well as horror, urban fantasy, and other genres.

My chief complaint is not that this has sci-fi elements, its that the Sci-Fi elements overshadow what, in my opinion, is a tangential use of the history theme. Given that the contest theme is history, this is a problem.

On top of that, the article just didn't do a lot to entertain me as a reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok. Fair. But. Is… is this not a sci-fi writing website? Have I been mislead in my interpretation of all these objects as works of science fiction?

I guess some of them are mostly just regular fiction huh. And there's not really too much space, or big nuclear stuff…

Huh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2k was sci-fi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Believe it or not I actually have no clue what the 2000 contest theme was? Not an argument against you just curious, I can't nail it down from the few articles I know were entries.

Since I was the one to create the object class I clearly disagree with you, but that's subjective. In my mind the idea of the Foundation being highly research oriented, yet not having an object class for things that they've explained but still need to contain is absurd.

Additionally in my personal opinion the use of object class as "containment difficulty" (and hence the three most common object classes currently) should absolutely be (slowly) replaced by the increasingly popular threat level mechanism… which I, uh. Didn't use here for some reason.

I think Godel works really well as an object class for a variety of reasons. It has a lot of potential for new and genre-breaking stories, where the focus isn't how unexplainable or extremely deadly an object is, but instead the story of the research that uncovers its mystery, and how that effects the Foundation, and their idea of how to contain an object, as well as their idea of what science is, and what the anomalous is.

I think it really fits in with the recent trend of new object classes that have to do with the foundation finding new ways to define what containment looks like and its different states, what an object is, in this case what it means for an object to be "explained" and how that relates to containment.

So like I said, subjective, but I personally think it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. Didn't get a whole lot out of this.

The new object class didn't particularly wow me, and I don't see it particularly catching on due to how niche it is.

On top of that, this article reads more like a 2000 Contest entry trying its best to be historically inclined. While the attachment to the contest theme is there, your focus on the science fiction aspect over the history aspect makes this entry seem a bit out of place compared to other entries that do a better job exploring the contest theme.

-1. Ultimately there wasn't a lot here that I took away. Combined with a tangential use of the contest theme and a luke warm new object class, I'm afraid its a downvote from me.

item.title: Re: Thank you for reading!
item.content:encoded:
+1 for great use of information theory and the new twist on Asimov! Autopoietic systems were what I got into after information theory, seems like you might get a kick out of them.

The ending felt a bit rushed, but otherwise stellar. Consider resubmitting after the contest!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ive run out of alphabets with good first and last letters to use for horrible things happening at this point.

Thanks for the kind words! I rewrote most of the portion of the end monologue that had to do with ordinals so Im glad it reads well now, as CWW said earlier in the thread the previous version was not well thought out and essentially just said the word infinity as many times as it could, not ideal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this, the layman skirting around ordinals was well done IMO and I'm a sucker for high concept with some math in it (and Asimov).

One thong though, you have a אZ-Class scenario, but these guys who won the doomsday contest used אK-Class scenario for apotheosis of mankind through anomalous means, which is almost opposite to your Z-Class scenario (end of all realities).

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Replying to myself to note that the above is incorrect: I confused epsilon with the number omega sub 1. The church-kleene ordinal is in fact unfathomably larger than epsilon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!
I definitely thought that for 4000 something grand and dramatic would be what I would want, so that's what I wrote!

I haven't read any of Heinlein, but I've heard very good things! This article was inspired in part by a short story by Asimov, and I know they were contemporaries

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This definitely gives me a wider idea of what I need to do differently on whatever I post next, thank you for going as into depth as you did.

I think the tone writing is definitely one of the things I have the most trouble with, I know how to do it in theory, but in practice I keep fudging it up, only more practice will help I guess.

Some of the other things you mention also come from me being unclear or poorly descriptive in my writing, for example the oracle section was actually not supposed to be foundation documentation at all, more like a personal archive, which is why I felt the drama and more personal signing could work there.

I'm working on a partial rewrite/expansion right now so maybe if you want to take a look and see if it changes your vote.

Also as for the bolding, that wasn't me, the articles tagged "4000" get autoposted on the 4k hub page, I'm guessing the format probably got broken by a special character in the person above me's title or name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tastily esoteric - though possibly too esoteric to get much traction. But also tastily profound, in its esotericness. Possibly dramatic, at the end there - but what can you do when the end is literally the grandest view possible?

Reminds me of some of Heinlein's goofier concepts and novels. A good work out for the part of the brain that groks concepts.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly I'm not entirely sure what this supposed to be. The idea that the Foundation created God seems unjustified in the writing. I think the article suffers from a lack of polish, namely a few proofreading problems, and the clinical tone seems off for a fair amount of the article. As already mentioned, Omega-K is already a thing. My headcanon of the Foundation isn't this… dramatic. I don't feel an O5 would sign a letter with an unofficial nickname, I don't think said unofficial nickname would appear in Foundation documentation. Other articles often suffer from a lot of build-up to a lackluster climax. I think this suffers the opposite. Were it not the 4000 contest, this'd be a no vote. As it is I think it's a -1.

Also, you've not bolded the name of the article in the 4000 contest hub page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw shit, I read that one and everything too, I should've remembered. Time to find a new, but similar greek letter. Maybe omicron? Looks a little bit like an o though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Omega-K scenario is already taken by these guys, maybe try something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it referees to different types of infinity? There's infinity א and infinity א0 in math for example. I don't think it works in this context though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didnt write it with that in mind, but the "dreaming the world" part definitely came to mind because ive had a small amount of education in regards to that mythology

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, Omega is basically Vishnu? I mean, it has potential, but I'm abstaining a vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah exactly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's like… a waypoint between "anomalous" and "explained", then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this, for the most part. Enough to upvote. Some things kind of lost me, as a non-science layperson — primarily the part with all the infinities. The point still came through just fine, but I did feel my eyes bouncing around the paragraph trying to lock on to the meaning.

Also don't think I really understood the concern from the sixth foundation, and how our universe almost destroyed all future ones. Maybe zero in on and flesh out that part? Highlighting the fragility of the happy ending would make it all the sweeter, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Im a little confused on this one because I thought I was fairly clear on it.

Its essentially a class for things that, the foundation has a scientific explaination for, but has to contain because it appears anomalous to anyone else, at least for the time being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Straight up, I did not understand a word of your esoteric class description, and downvoted immediately. If you can't even justify it in-universe, I don't see a need for it. The rest of the article did nothing to suggest I change my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oof yikes, that one hurts. Sorry I doubted your knowledge there. A fair enough critique I suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right, it doesn't have to mean anything. To be evocative of an idea, it needs to be well-written. It is not well-written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know what infinity is, and I know what ordinal numbers are. It's fine to have them in this article. The passage I quoted doesn't have anything to do with ordinals; in fact, it says nothing that simply identifying the sequence of universes as "infinite" does not. It's just the word "infinite", repeated a few times, with a little glue to hold it together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I completely agree with everything you have to say here. When writing the -C section my goal was essentially to try and add an element of additional horror or interest, or just something meatier to the baseline concept of "The last question by Isaac Asimov but it keeps going".

Overall I think as it stands it feels somewhat like an afterthought, and Isn't explained very well as you said. over the next day or two I'm going to try and re-write this section in a way that makes it more coherent, and understandable as part of the rest of the story.

P.S As for the grammatical errors, I freeze up in fear every time I have to type it it's or its, and I still somehow get it wrong 3/4 of the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ordinal_number Checkmate atheists.

In all seriousness though I was somewhat afraid that was something a lot of people would not understand from either a conceptual or storytelling point of view, but I thought it was important to have in there considering it's where the idea for the whole skip has its genesis. The idea being that as sapient life forms became more and more advanced, they would figure out ways to more quickly advance themselves, leading to infinite series of worlds.

The theme here is recursion, 1 plus 1 is 2, 2 plus 1 is three. three whole multiverses in this case. How far can we take this idea? Mathematics says we can take it much, much farther than expected, until the "1, 2, 3…" infinity we're used to seems incredibly small. The wikipedia article takes it as far as Infinity to the power of infinity, but the implication of the last portion of this skip is that it goes as far as it can, to a number known as the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Church%E2%80%93Kleene_ordinal

Again that was all just my conceptualization, and I'm not sure if it should be taken into account when judging the thing in itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it doesn't have to literally mean anything to be evocative of an idea though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These infinite chains continued, until they too were unending. Infinity times infinity. Still no end in sight, we saw infinite infinities, infinite chains of infinite chains, each process being taken to its limit until it wasn't limits being taken to infinity, but infinite limits being taken.

I'm like 90% sure that this literally does not mean anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I figured Omega was just a better name for storytelling. And I was wondering about the C-K ordinal, given your Godel references, so that one makes more sense (if you care about such things).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disregard this post, thought you meant something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Omega name was an attempt to find a middle ground between the actual use of the greek alphabet poetically, with Omega representing "the end", being at the end of the article, and the end of the series of univeres leading to the end, and the actual use of Omega in ordinal arithmetic were it is the first infinite cardinal/ordinal.

Fun fact: the spot in the series of ordinals I am referencing here is not actual epsilon, but a much smaller one: the Church-Kleene Ordinal, which is the smallest non-recursive ordinal, and the set of all recursive ordinals. Hence it being a "limit of infinities"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to upvote this hard, because the idea is so cool and the first part of the article is so well done. I don't think I can yet, though, because of how many unclear statements, grammatical issues, misplaced words, and generally confusing sentence structures there are in some of the latter sections.

+ examples
- examples

Following SCP-4000-B's demanifestation, MUORG's ongoing monitoring and research program led to the discovery of SCP-4000-C. SCP-4000-C is a meta-universal space, with properties similar to our own, equipped with a form of iterative intelligence (designated SCP-4000-C-1) able to alter the spaces inherent structure. Research indicates that in addition to our own multiverse, SCP-4000-A created this space, though no contact can be made with them to confirm this, and the space itself is shielded from outside contact.

I can't extract much understanding from this paragraph. Where is this space? Parallel to our reality, off in some conceptual dimension, or more like a pocket dimension that's local to a particular area of our universe? What kind of iterative intelligence is it that occupies this space? Is it a physical object, or a pattern of ideas, or what?
I also notice an ongoing issue with apostrophes - remember that "its" is possessive and "it's" is short for "it is", and many possessives like "space's" are missing apostrophes.

An entity (designated SCP-4000-C-2) resides in an isolated portion of SCP-4000-C, which is unable to be affected by SCP-4000-C-1. SCP-4000-C-2 closely resembles the cognitive of SCP-4000-A members, but a portion has been grafted to it's cognitive structure resembling that of a human being.

I really don't understand what's going on here. What do "closely resembles the cognitive of SCP-4000-A members" or "grafted to its cognitive structure" mean?

Between the dates of 4/13/2039 and 4/28/2039 a series of changes took place inside SCP-4000-C. MUORG researchers discovered two human consciousness had been introduced to the structure. These humans progressed through SCP-4000-C over the course of the following weeks, reaching the portion containing SCP-4000-C-2, at which point they rested for two hours and interacted with the entity. Following this, at the time of 16:43:22, on 4/28/2039, SCP-4000-C-2 created a self-perpetuating, infovorous singularity which consumed the entirety of SCP-4000-C.

This is a bit more coherent to me than the previous paragraphs, but it seems to assume a few fundamental ideas about the nature of 4000-C that are completely unstated. I'm still left pretty helpless to understand what's actually happening here, and it really seems like a lot of information that the Foundation should have put in the article isn't here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very math nerdy question: why did you decide to call your final object Omega, rather than its usual name, Epsilon?

Fun piece in any case!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Y'know you'd really think the 3 distinct, individual proofreads I did last night would have caught that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really enjoyed this! Addendum 4000-2 seems to be missing a word though:
"…reaching the portion containing SCP-4000-C-2, at which point they rested for two hours interacted with the entity."
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should've known a bunch of sci-fi writers would get the reference but I was honestly worried no one would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is insufficient data for a meaningful reference. +1.

item.title: Thank you for reading!
item.content:encoded:
This is my very first post on the site!
Special thanks to undergroundmonorail for proofreading and tone critique!

I should probably have a much longer list than that for people that took a look at it, but I had a concept I was really excited about, I polished it up, and I'm confident that it isn't complete garbage.

Thank you for reading, and thank so many of you for being on the forums and in the chat to talk to me while I worked on this!

Edit, 7/15/18: significant rewrite/expansion made! Consider changing your vote!
